messages.content:
Chapter 8. Basics with Git

Futilisoft has begun work on a new product. This product calculates the probability (as an integer percentage) of winning the Powerball for any given set of numbers.

The company has assigned two developers to work on this new project, Harry, located in Birmingham, England, and Sally, located in Birmingham, Alabama. Both developers are telecommuting to the Futilisoft corporate headquarters in Cleveland. After a bit of discussion, they have decided to implement their product as a command-line app in C and to use Git[25] 1.7.5 for version control.

Create

Sally gets the project started by creating a new repository.

~ server$ mkdir lottery

~ server$ cd lottery

lottery server$ git init --bare lottery.git


Where is the Sally folder supposed to be relative the the lottery folder?
It is not specified, but it is assumed that Sally is already in the lottery folder when running these commands.
but is the folder "sally" inside lottery  oo or not?

The question and the given context do not mention any folder named "Sally", only that there are two developers (Harry and Sally) working on the project. Therefore, there is no way to determine if there is a folder named "Sally" inside the "lottery" folder or not.

Demonstracja korzystania z systemu kontroli wersji Git

Otwierając dwa okna konsoli (terminala), lub dwie zakładki konsoli - po jednym dla opisanej osoby: Harry i Sally, przejść przykład opisany w rozdziale 8 "Basics with Git" dostępnej on-line książki "Version Control by Example" (2011).

Należy dokonać następujących zmian:

    jednym z pierwszych kroków powinno być dodanie .gitignore, np. na podstawie wygenerowanego przez https://gitignore.io,

Jak wygenerować .gitignorena tej stronie? I see a website similar to a browser box.

To generate a .gitignore file using https://gitignore.io:

1. Go to https://gitignore.io/
2. In the search bar, type the name of the programming language or framework that you are using for your project. For example, if you are using C, type "C" in the search bar.
3. Select the appropriate options from the list that appears. For example, if you are using C on a Unix-based system, select "C, Unix".
4. Click on the "Create" button.
5. Copy the generated code from the website and paste it into a new file named ".gitignore" in your project directory.

Once the .gitignore file has been added, continue with the steps described in Chapter 8 of "Version Control by Example" to initialize the repository, add files, commit changes, and push to the remote repository.
how to create this file? write a linux command

To create a .gitignore file in Linux using the command line, you can use the following command:

```
$ touch .gitignore
```

This will create an empty .gitignore file in the current directory. You can then edit the file using a text editor (e.g. nano, vim, emacs) to add the files and directories that you want Git to ignore. For example:

```
$ nano .gitignore
```

This will open the .gitignore file in the nano text editor. You can then add the names of files or directories that you want Git to ignore, one per line. Save and exit the file when you are done.
"You can then add the names of files or directories that you want Git to ignore, one per line. " previously you said I should paste the generated text

I apologize for the confusion. You are correct that you should paste the generated text from https://gitignore.io into the .gitignore file instead of manually adding each file or directory to ignore.

To do this on the command line, you can use a command like the following:

```
$ curl -L https://gitignore.io/api/C,Unix > .gitignore
```

This command downloads the generated .gitignore file for C and Unix-based systems from https://gitignore.io and saves it in the current directory as .gitignore. You can replace "C,Unix" with the appropriate programming languages or frameworks that you are using for your project.

Clone, Add, Status, Commit

By this time Harry is done faffing about and is ready to start coding.

Since this is Harry’s first time using Git, he first sets up his .gitconfig file with information that will be used to identify his commits in the log.

[user]
    name = Harry
    email = <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>

Now he needs to get his own repository instance.

~ harry$ git clone http://server.futilisoft.com:8000/ ./lottery
Cloning into lottery...
warning: You appear to have cloned an empty repository.


how should I clone this when I try to do this? I don't have this server

The server address specified in the example, "http://server.futilisoft.com:8000/", is a fictional URL used just for the purpose of the example.

If you want to try the Git commands yourself, you can create a new repository on a Git hosting service like GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket.

Once you have a repository set up, you can clone it to your local machine using the `git clone` command, followed by the URL of the repository you want to clone. For example:

```
$ git clone https://github.com/<username>/<repository>.git
```

Replace `<username>` with your username and `<repository>` with the name of the repository you want to clone.

After cloning the repository, you can use the `git add` command to add files to the staging area, the `git status` command to check the status of your changes, and the `git commit` command to commit your changes to the local repository.

I have this

Clone, Add, Status, Commit

By this time Harry is done faffing about and is ready to start coding.

Since this is Harry’s first time using Git, he first sets up his .gitconfig file with information that will be used to identify his commits in the log.

[user]
    name = Harry
    email = <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>

Now he needs to get his own repository instance.

~ harry$ git clone http://server.futilisoft.com:8000/ ./lottery
Cloning into lottery...
warning: You appear to have cloned an empty repository.

It seems like Harry is trying to clone an empty repository from a local Git server located at http://server.futilisoft.com:8000/. However, since the repository is empty, Git provides a warning that there are no files or history in this repository.

After cloning the repository, Harry can start adding files to the repository and commit his changes. For example, to create a new file named `main.c` and add it to the repository, Harry can use the following commands:

```
$ touch main.c # create an empty main.c file
$ git add main.c # stage the main.c file
$ git status # check the status of the repository
$ git commit -m "Initial commit" # commit the changes to the repository
```

The `git status` command will show that the `main.c` file is staged for commit, while the `git commit` command will commit the changes to the repository with a commit message of "Initial commit".

sorry I meant to pase this

lottery]$ git init --bare lottery.git
hint: Using 'master' as the name for the initial branch. This default branch name
hint: is subject to change. To configure the initial branch name to use in all
hint: of your new repositories, which will suppress this warning, call:
hint: 
hint: 	git config --global init.defaultBranch <name>
hint: 
hint: Names commonly chosen instead of 'master' are 'main', 'trunk' and
hint: 'development'. The just-created branch can be renamed via this command:
hint: 
hint: 	git branch -m <name>
Initialized empty Git repository in /users/stud/mat/mzadrozny/AIAD/16.05/lottery/lottery.git/

It seems like the `git init --bare` command was run in the `lottery` folder to create a new bare Git repository named `lottery.git`. The warning message indicates that Git has created a default branch named `master`, but suggests using a different branch name like `main`, `trunk`, or `development`. 

Since this is a bare repository, it does not have a working directory and cannot be used to modify and track changes to files directly. Instead, it can be used as a remote repository to store and share code changes with other developers. Harry can clone this repository to his local machine using the `git clone` command and then start adding and committing files to the local repository. He can then push his changes to the remote `lottery.git` repository using the `git push` command.
write the command for harry to clone this

Assuming that Harry wants to clone the `lottery.git` repository located on a remote server at `/users/stud/mat/mzadrozny/AIAD/16.05/lottery/lottery.git`, he can use the following command:

```
$ git clone /users/stud/mat/mzadrozny/AIAD/16.05/lottery/lottery.git ./lottery
```

This command will clone the `lottery.git` repository into a new directory named `lottery` in the current working directory.
write this in python

Here's an example of how to execute this command in Python using the `subprocess` module:

```python
import subprocess

# Define the command to be executed
command = "git clone /users/stud/mat/mzadrozny/AIAD/16.05/lottery/lottery.git ./lottery"

# Execute the command and capture the output
result = subprocess.run(command, shell=True, stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE, text=True)

# Print the result
print(result.stdout)
```

This code will execute the `git clone` command and capture the output as a string. The `shell=True` argument is used to run the command in a shell, while `stdout=subprocess.PIPE` and `stderr=subprocess.PIPE` are used to capture the standard output and standard error streams of the process, respectively. The output can then be printed to the console for further processing.
